chapters.title: The mind and body are not two separate things
chapters.text: French thinker René Descartes argued that the mind stood apart from the body, working without any physical pull. People accepted this view for years and called it Cartesian Dualism. They labeled the mind as immaterial and the body as material. The body is not whole without the mind, and the mind is not whole without the body. Research by Antonio Damasio has shown the opposite. The mind is in fact shaped by the body. When people suffer heavy damage to their frontal lobes, their outward behavior often shifts in striking ways. Damage to the frontal lobe of the brain has been linked to a number of odd shifts in behavior. People who were once cool and steady can swing to extremes. Doctors call this the Gage syndrome, named for the famous railroad foreman Phineas Gage. He survived a brutal accident in which an iron rod shot through his left eye and out the base of his skull, tearing through much of his frontal lobe. The brain is the home of the mind, and it must be shielded from harm or both mind and body will pay. Before the accident, Phineas Gage was warm, social, and quick witted. He did not lose his motor skills, but he became a different man. Mood swings and bursts of anger made it hard to hold a job, and a man who had lived in comfort found himself in poverty by age 25. The self is a state the body keeps rebuilding, as Antonio Damasio has written.

chapters.title: The brain runs a set of networks that support its work
chapters.text: Much of what we know about the brain comes from neuroanatomy, the study of the structure and layout of the nervous system. We now know that the brain has separate, segregated, and defined systems. That means we can read a person behavior by studying their brain activity. We know only a little of how the brain works, but that little can guide us in reading the body. With Gage syndrome, there is no quick way to tie a specific behavior to a clear part of the brain. There is no big shift on the outside, so you have to watch the new traits show up in daily life before you can be sure a change has happened. Physical changes from frontal lobe damage are often missed because they are subtle. The brain is a system of smaller systems, and each one can be used to track a change in a person. The Gage case happened at a time when deep brain study was hard, so it was set aside. Modern tools in neuroanatomy have made it possible to spot and treat many brain conditions. His case became a reference point for studies to this day and a spark for the work of Damasio, who set out to challenge the Descartes view. He wanted to trace a physical change, such as an injury, to a clear shift in mood or behavior.

chapters.title: Emotions are not the enemy of reason but shape our choices
chapters.text: Neuroanatomy also teaches us about emotion and reason, two forces that often pull against each other and shape how we decide. We tend to think emotions cloud our judgment, but in fact they help us choose well. How we act is shaped by how we think, a theme that runs through both philosophy and science. Descartes is known for the line I think, therefore I am, which also frames the person as driven by the mind. It is striking that Descartes himself saw the mind as a thing apart from the body. Most of what we do, on purpose or not, is shaped by emotion. We should also admit that much of what we do comes from either emotion or reason. But are they truly separate. They are not. The set of brain systems that handle clear thought also handle feeling. The brain does not split emotion and reason into separate parts, both rise from the same networks. Plenty of emotion can muddy reason and cloud our view. That is the case when it runs hot, or so we thought. It turns out that too little emotion can also throw reason off. In cases of frontal lobe damage, a person loses most of their empathy, which often leads to poor choices.

chapters.title: Reason and feeling meet to give us a clearer view
chapters.text: Damasio put forward a theory to explain the science of rationality. He points to four factors that shape how we handle the issue. First, choices in complex settings call for clear reasoning strategies. Second, feelings play a key role in the body biological regulation. Third, the brain builds and rebuilds many images. Fourth, the brain can hold those images through a large store of memory. These factors come together to shape how we read the world. How we do that rests on the link between reason and choice, and on the link between feelings and emotions. It is the meeting of feeling and reason that gives us sound and clear headed choices. The two are often used as if they were the same, but they are not. Feelings are a complex process, trickier to pull apart. Feelings are the body side of a conscious process, a way the body reacts. Emotions, on the other hand, are the lived experience of those body reactions. Feelings are full, complex states, while emotions are how those states show up in us. In other words, feelings give shape to emotion, and without feelings we would not grasp our own emotions. There are two kinds of emotion, primary and secondary. Primary emotions are the primal ones, like fear and worry, that rise from the amygdala. Primary emotions are the base reactions we evolved to face daily life. Secondary emotions rise from a more measured process. Instead of a quick flash, they come from the link between close observation and the first primary emotion. The human mind can feel two clashing emotions at the same time and can sense many physical feelings too, and the brain can read them all with surprising skill.

chapters.title: We can map our reactions through somatic markers
chapters.text: The secondary emotional process matters a lot when it comes to avoiding rash choices that would happen without it. Secondary emotional pathways are those where feeling and reason both shape the final call. If we lean only on primary emotions, we tend to make worse choices most of the time. The role of feeling in choice is often missed because many think feelings get in the way of clear thinking. But without feelings, there is no real reasoning in our choices. So the partnership has to work if we want clear and steady ways to express what we feel. Reason feels hollow if it is not backed by a clear expression of feeling. The key to this union is the somatic marker. Somatic markers are feelings tied to emotions directly, often paired with body signs like a racing heart or sweat. Think of them as the cues that tell you when a strong primary emotion is running hot, or not. The body side of emotion helps shape choices that can be quick or slow. Somatic markers do more than steer choices. They also act as a kind of signal for reward and pleasure. Patients with frontal lobe damage or other brain injury pay less attention to reward and satisfaction when they do a task. People with heavy frontal lobe damage show little activity in the reward center. They carry out tasks as part of a routine without any real pleasure. The same pattern shows up in some people with psychopathy, where the cause may be unusual growth in that brain region during fetal life. It is more proof that the mind shapes the body and the body shapes the mind.

chapters.title: The Descartes error showed us the link between mind and body
chapters.text: So what was the Descartes error, and how does Damasio try to fix it. In short, René Descartes was sure that human beings exist as a working pair. He called it a metaphysical pair, meaning the mind and body, feeling and reason, are together and yet apart. The pair of mind and body is one that both sets them apart and ties them together. We have learned that this is hardly true. Much of what the body does, on purpose or not, is steered by emotion. Social shifts in people can be traced to a brain change caused by injury or by what did or did not happen in the brain during fetal growth. The mind plays a key role in how the body works, and any flaw in one shows up in the other. Think of the mind as the software that runs on the hardware, which is the body. Without the body, the mind is a floating thing with no anchor. Without the mind, the body is a lifeless shell. The mind and body are a true pair, but unlike the Descartes view, they are linked and always shaping each other, with neither in charge. The mind is the driver and the body is the vehicle, and both need each other to work well. In metaphysics, you can in theory pull the mind away from the body. That may have fed the metaphysical pair idea, since people once thought of humans as a sum of two parts. Metaphysics does not fully grasp the mind body pair, because it sets aside their dependence. The error was to treat body and mind as separate. Damasio set out to argue that the mind is embodied, meaning it has full form inside and with the body. Although Damasio meant to correct the error, he ended up linking his view to the one he was correcting.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text: The place of neuroanatomy in modern life is hard to overstate, since it gives us a window into how the brain works with the body. The old view was that the brain and mind stood apart from the body, but that does not hold up. It would mean the mind could walk off and come back when it felt like it. That might pass in metaphysics, but not in real life, since there is no scientific proof and since Damasio has done so much research in neuroanatomy and neurology. He argues that mind and body work in a smooth unity, not apart. The body acts and works in direct response to the mind, and the body balance called homeostasis leans on the mind neural network. The mind is just a soft entity that comes to life through the physical web of nerves, muscles, and neurons in the brain. With that in mind, neither can work without the other, and the behavior of one always shows up in the other. The Descartes error was to see either as a stand alone thing, and Damasio fixed that by showing they depend on each other. Try this. The mind and body are basically one, so it pays to take care of both. You can try meditation and physical exercise to bring them into better tune. If you read to the end, well done.
